next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

Nita woke to the scrape of scissors on construction paper.

The apartment was still dark in the corners, radiator hissing like it had a private grievance with the wall. She had slept for maybe ninety minutes in two broken pieces. The browser tabs from last night were still open on her phone: the co-op assessment portal, Ben's card balance, Mina's transfer from three weeks ago with the note `For the windows`, as if a lie told in parentheticals counted less. Her eyes found the kitchen table and stayed there.

Leela sat cross-legged on one of the dining chairs in her school leggings, blue glasses sliding down her nose, trimming blue arrows out of construction paper with the concentration of a bomb technician. Her fingers were streaked navy from a leaking marker. Ben stood at the counter in yesterday's T-shirt, peeling district logos off the edge of a cardboard trifold because Leela had pasted them on crooked and now wanted them "less sponsored."

"You should eat something before we go," Nita said.

Leela did not look up. "I had a banana."

"Half a banana," Ben said. "The rest became engineering material."

On the table beside the scissors was the Future Citizens Week packet from Sunnyside Interop Academy, printed on thin paper that had already fuzzed at the folds. FAMILY PARTICIPATION STRONGLY ENCOURAGED, the header said. Students will present a household system, service role, or civic routine that contributes to our shared future. A blue sidebar invited proxy households to offer "real-world examples of embodied civic trust." Sponsors' logos ran along the bottom...
open_hooks_to_carry: Ben's debt must feel embarrassing in concrete terms rather than merely numerical.
Nita's borrowed money from her mother reveals her own private maintenance of the family facade.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...the mug as if answers might have settled there with the leaves. "But I'm sad in a different way now."

Ben wrapped both hands around his own tea. "Yeah."

"Not less sad."

"No."

"Just less stupid."

He nodded. There was nothing to improve in that.

The power came back without warning. The overhead light flashed on so white it made both of them squint. The refrigerator shuddered, coughed, and restarted. Down the hall, the apartment hub gave a polite waking chime that sounded obscene after the dark. Ben's cobalt phone lit first, then buzzed itself against the counter in a new fit of relevance. Nita's device followed with its cooler, more discreet insistence.

Neither of them picked one up.

In the other room, Leela made a sleep-thick noise and said, "Did it remember?"

Ben looked at Nita under the rude electric light. The steam from her mug had gone visible and ordinary now. Every tired line was back. Every object in the kitchen had returned to being itself instead of a silhouette. Nothing had become easier. The sink still held two unwashed forks. The branded grocery bags still waited by the door. Morning was coming with all its routes and prices attached.

Nita set her mug down and, with the side of one finger, wiped a streak of dried wax from the back of his hand. It was such a small thing that it might have been habit. It was not habit anymore.

"Yeah," she called toward Leela's room, not loudly. "It remembered."

Ben stood very still after that, his phone buzzing a second time against the counter and going unanswered, while the apartment filled itself back up with noise.
state_deltas_required: Bills, debt, and status upkeep force the couple to confront the private lies each has told in order to keep the household legible as stable.
Financial secrecy breaks the fragile alliance and makes freedom look like real class loss rather than a brave abstraction.
